name: Lyft Ride Matching
overview:
The Ride Matching Service pairs ride requests with available drivers. It must find the best driver for each
request within 30 seconds, considering proximity, driver rating, vehicle type, and surge pricing zones. The
service must handle thousands of simultaneous ride requests, prevent double-booking of drivers, and gracefully
handle driver cancellations by re-matching the rider. Matching quality directly impacts rider wait time and driver
utilization.

inScope: Match rider request to best available driver within 30 seconds
Consider proximity, driver rating, vehicle type
Prevent double-booking (one driver per ride at a time)
Re-match if driver cancels within 2 minutes

items: Matching decision within 30 seconds; matching computation <500ms
Strong consistency for driver assignment — no double-booking under any concurrency
99.99% availability — matching outages directly prevent rides from starting
Re-matching on driver cancellation must complete within 30 seconds
Horizontally scalable: matching throughput scales with Kafka partitions and worker instances

entities.name: RideRequest
entities.schema:
{
  request_id: UUID,
  rider_id: string,
  pickup: { lat, lng },
  dropoff: { lat, lng },
  vehicle_type: enum(STANDARD|XL|LUX),

  status: enum(SEARCHING|MATCHED|CANCELLED|COMPLETED),
  created_at: timestamp,
  matched_driver_id: string (nullable)
}

approaches.title: Nearest Driver (Greedy)
approaches.description: Always assign the geographically nearest available driver. Simple but suboptimal. The nearest driver may have a low rating or be heading away from the pickup. Doesn't consider driver utilization across the city.
approaches.tradeoff: Suboptimal matching quality; ignores driver rating and direction.

approaches.title: Scoring Function
approaches.description: Score each candidate driver: score = w1×(1/distance) + w2×rating + w3×direction_alignment. Better quality than pure proximity. But evaluating all drivers in a large radius is expensive. Requires tuning weights.
approaches.tradeoff: Expensive to evaluate all candidates; weight tuning is empirical.

approaches.title: Two-Phase Matching with Distributed Lock
approaches.description: Phase 1: Find top 5 candidate drivers using Redis GEORADIUS (fast proximity filter). Phase 2: Score candidates using the scoring function. Assign the highest-scoring driver. Driver assignment uses a Redis distributed lock (SETNX on driver_id) to prevent double-booking. If the lock fails (driver already assigned), move to the next candidate. If no driver found in 30 seconds, expand search radius by 50% and retry.
approaches.tradeoff: Redis lock adds latency; lock TTL must be tuned; expanding radius may match suboptimal

approaches.title: drivers.
approaches.description: Handling Driver Cancellations No Re-matching If a driver cancels, the rider must submit a new request. Poor user experience. Rider loses their place in the queue.
approaches.tradeoff: Poor UX; rider must restart the process.

approaches.title: Automatic Re-matching with Priority Queue
approaches.description: If a driver cancels within 2 minutes of matching, the ride request is re-queued with HIGH priority. The matching service processes HIGH priority requests first. The rider is notified of the cancellation and shown a new ETA. Drivers who cancel frequently are penalized in the scoring function.
approaches.tradeoff: Re-matching adds wait time; rider experience degrades with multiple cancellations.

components.name: Ride Request API
components.responsibility: Accepts ride requests; validates; publishes to Kafka matching queue

components.name: Matching Service
components.responsibility: Consumes ride requests; queries Redis Geo for candidates; scores and assigns

components.name: Redis Geo
components.responsibility: Geospatial index of available drivers; updated by Driver Location Service

components.name: Redis Locks
components.responsibility: Distributed locks per driver_id to prevent double-booking

components.name: Kafka
components.responsibility: Ride request queue (HIGH/NORMAL priority); driver status events

components.name: Ride DB (PostgreSQL)
components.responsibility: Stores ride records; source of truth for ride state

components.name: Notification Service
components.responsibility: Notifies driver of new ride request; notifies rider of match

components.name: ETA Service
components.responsibility: Computes driver-to-pickup ETA for matching score

questions.title: 2. Driver Acceptance Rate
questions.content:
Drivers can accept or reject ride requests. If a driver rejects, the matching service moves to the next candidate.
To minimize rejection rates, the matching service only sends requests to drivers who are likely to accept (based
on historical acceptance rate, current location relative to pickup, and time since last ride). Drivers with <80%
acceptance rate are deprioritized in the scoring function.

questions.title: 3. Surge Zone Awareness
questions.content:
During surge pricing, the matching service prioritizes drivers in surge zones to maximize driver earnings and
reduce wait times. The scoring function adds a surge_bonus term for drivers in high-demand zones. This
incentivizes drivers to move to surge zones, which helps balance supply and demand.

questions.title: 4. Batch Matching for Efficiency
questions.content:
Instead of matching one ride at a time, the matching service batches ride requests that arrive within a 500ms
window and solves a global assignment problem (Hungarian algorithm) to maximize total matching quality. This
is more efficient than greedy matching and reduces the number of Redis lock contentions.

questions.title: 5. Fallback to Manual Dispatch
questions.content:
If no driver is found after 3 matching attempts (90 seconds), the ride request is escalated to a manual dispatch
queue where a human operator can intervene. This handles edge cases like remote pickup locations or unusual
vehicle type requests.

keyFlows: ① Rider requests ride → Ride API → Kafka matching queue
② Matching Svc: GEORADIUS → top 5 candidates from Redis Geo
③ Score candidates (proximity + rating + ETA); acquire Redis lock on driver
④ Lock acquired → write match to Ride DB → notify driver + rider
⑤ Driver cancels → re-queue with HIGH priority → re-match

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study the Hungarian algorithm for optimal assignment: how it solves the assignment problem
in O(n^3) time, and how it's applied to batch ride matching.
Expert Note: Learn about Redis distributed locks: the SETNX + EXPIRE pattern, the Redlock algorithm for
multi-node safety, and why lock TTL must exceed the critical section duration.

Expert Note: Understand the explore-exploit trade-off in matching: how to balance matching quality (exploit
best driver) with exploration (try new drivers to learn their acceptance patterns).
Expert Note: Study Kafka consumer group rebalancing: how partition assignment changes when matching
service instances are added/removed, and how to minimize rebalancing latency.
Expert Note: Learn about the two-sided marketplace dynamics in ride-sharing: how driver and rider
supply/demand imbalances affect matching quality, and how surge pricing restores equilibrium.
